macro_segments.title: The Value Requirement for Redeeming a Firstborn Donkey with a Sheep

micro_segments.title: Opening: The Debate over Rabbi Yehuda and Rabbi Shimon Regarding Benefit Status of the Donkey
The Problem of Benefit: How Can a Forbidden Object Have Monetary Value for Redemption?
Resolving the Problem Through Rabbi Yose b'Rabbi Yehuda's Minimum Shekel Requirement
Two Redemptions in the Verses: Immediate Redemption and Minimal Value Redemption
The Concern: Might the Donkey Redemption Follow the Rules of Human Firstborn (30 Days, 5 Shekels)?
The Sources of the Minimum Shekel Requirement: Rabbi Yose b'Rabbi Yehuda's Position
The Dilemma: Either Compare to Humans (5 Shekels) or Explain the Shekel Requirement Otherwise
No Comparison to Humans: Rabbi Yose b'Rabbi Yehuda Learns the Shekel from the Erech Verse
The Erech Verse and Its Principle: All Valuations Must Be at Least a Shekel
The Rabbis' Counterargument: The Erech Verse Applies Only to Poverty Adjustments
Ruling: The Halacha Follows the Sages, Allowing Even Minimal Value (Kol Shehu)
What is the Practical Minimum? A Scrawny Sheep Worth a Danka
The Implicit Teaching of the Mishnah: Big or Small Sheep Proves No Minimum Requirement

macro_segments.title: The Practical Guidelines for Redemption: The Range from Stingy to Generous

micro_segments.title: The Case of Rav Yehuda Nesia: Seeking Rav Tarfon's Guidance on Donkey Redemption
Rav Tarfon's Answer: A Range of Appropriate Values Based on Character
The Coins: Sela (4 Zuzim), Shekel (2 Zuzim), and Bargaya (3 Zuzim) as Practical Standards
Rava's Ruling: The Standard Practice Should Be Bargaya (3 Zuzim, the Middle Ground)
Resolving the Apparent Contradiction: Between Kol Shehu and the Bargaya Standard
The Distinction: Bedieved (Done on Your Own) vs. L'chat (Advice from Sages)

macro_segments.title: Alternative Methods of Redemption: Value-Based Redemption Without a Sheep

micro_segments.title: Rav Yitzchak Citing Reish Lakish: Redemption by Value if No Sheep is Available
The Question of Attribution: Is This Ruling According to Rabbi Shimon or Rabbi Yehuda?
The Problem: We Rule Like Rabbi Yehuda, Not Rabbi Shimon
Resolution: The Ruling Works for Both Opinions via the Hekdesh Analogy
The Hekdesh Comparison: Sanctified Objects Can Be Redeemed by Value, So Too the Donkey
The Torah's Intent: The Sheep Requirement Was to Make It Easier, Not Harder

macro_segments.title: Who Can Redeem and Who Receives the Donkey: The Doctrine of Ownership

micro_segments.title: The Case of Rav Nachman b'Rav Yitzchak: Redeeming with Herbs at Full Value
The Question: Can a Third Party Redeem Someone Else's Firstborn Donkey?
Initial Ruling: Yes, Third Party Redemption Works and the Sheep Goes to the Kohen
The Follow-Up Question: Who Gets to Keep the Redeemed Donkey—Redeemer or Owner?
According to Rabbi Shimon: Owner Keeps Donkey (It Was Never Forbidden in Benefit)
According to Rabbi Yehuda: The Problem—Is the Donkey Like Hekdesh, Out of Owner's Hands?
The Two Sub-Possibilities: Does Owner Have Benefit Interest Due to Redemption Discount?
Evidence from Theft Law: The Burglar Pays Double, Showing Owner Retains Monetary Interest
The Proof from Scripture: The Double Payment is Only from a Person's House, Not from Hekdesh
Conclusion: Not Like Hekdesh—Owner Retains Ownership and Benefits from Redemption Discount

macro_segments.title: The Doubtful Firstborn Donkey: Redemption Without Obligation to Give to Kohen

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah: Taking Maaser from Sheep Used to Redeem Doubtful Firstborn Donkeys
The Problem: Why Would You Take Maaser from Sheep Not Born to the Kohen?
The Scenario: A Jew with Ten Doubtful Firstborn Donkeys Separates Sheep and Takes Maaser
The Teaching: Even Though You Keep the Sheep, You Still Separate Maaser Behemah
Why Maaser Still Applies: The Sheep Were Born to You and You Keep Them

macro_segments.title: The Inheritance Through a Kohen: Exemption from Obligation to Give to a Kohen

micro_segments.title: The Scenario: Definite Firstborn Donkeys Inherited Through a Kohen Grandfather
The Law: A Kohen is Exempt from Giving Firstborn Donkeys to a Kohen
The Status Change: When Inherited by a Yisrael Descendant, the Obligation to Give Remains Waived
The Practical Resolution: Redeem with Sheep and Keep Them Without Obligation to Kohen

macro_segments.title: Parallel Case: Terumot and Maasrot from Grain Inherited Through a Kohen

micro_segments.title: The Scenario: Grain Inherited Through a Kohen Grandfather (Structural Parallel to Donkeys)
The Explanation: It's as If the Grandfather Already Separated Terumot and Maasrot
Why Two Cases Are Needed: The Donkey Case Shows Clear Independence; Grain Case Requires Proof

macro_segments.title: Tevel from a Non-Jew: The Principle of Non-Jewish Ownership and Exemption

micro_segments.title: The Case: Purchasing Winnowed Tevel from a Non-Jew and Separating Maaser
The Ruling: You Separate Maaser But Get to Keep It (Do Not Give to Kohen)
The Principle: Non-Jewish Ownership Does Not Exempt Grain From Terumot and Maasrot
The Rationale: You Obtained It From Someone Who Could Never Be Brought to Beis Din

macro_segments.title: The Debate Over Swapped Grain and the Exception for Terumah Gedolah vs. Terumas Maaser

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah: Grain Left With Non-Jew is Treated as Possibly Swapped
The Dispute: Does Assumption of Swap Require Giving to Kohen (Tanna Kamma) or Only Separating (Rabbi Shimon)?
The Contradiction: We Said You Keep Tevel From Non-Jew, But Here You'd Have to Give It
The Resolution: The Exception Applies Only to Terumas Maaser, Not Terumah Gedolah
The Source: Verse Distinguishes Between Taking from Israelites (Give Terumas Maaser) vs. Non-Jews (Don't Give)
Why Grain Must Still Be Separated: The Principle of How You Come Into Possession Affects Obligation

macro_segments.title: Final Mishnah: What Happens if the Sheep Dies Before Being Given to the Kohen?

micro_segments.title: The Case: Sheep Dies in Owner's Possession After Redemption But Before Kohen Receives It
The Ruling: Kohen Can Still Benefit From Dead Sheep; Owner's Obligation is Complete
The Proof: As Soon as Owner Separated the Sheep, Kohen Takes Possession in Principle
The Implication: Owner Makes Blessing at Redemption, Not at Handing Over to Kohen

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Yehuda vs. Rabbi Shimon (Benefit Status)
